natural_occurrence.description: THIS is what makes bacon PINK! Without it, bacon would be gray/brown. Prevents deadly botulism (Clostridium botulinum) - one of the most potent toxins known. Called 'pink salt' or 'curing salt' (Prague Powder #1) when mixed with regular salt. Has been used for meat preservation for thousands of years (before chemistry was understood). The pink color is from nitric oxide combining with myoglobin in meat. Also adds characteristic 'cured meat' flavor. Represents the classic food additive dilemma: known risks vs. known benefits - prevents acute botulism BUT linked to long-term cancer risk when forming nitrosamines.

manufacturing.description: Produced industrially by reacting nitric acid with sodium hydroxide or sodium carbonate, followed by neutralization and crystallization.

other_uses.industrial: Used in metal treatment, dye production, and as corrosion inhibitor
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Vasodilator in some medical applications; antidote for cyanide poisoning

eu.conditions: STRICTLY REGULATED; maximum residual levels enforced; often combined with ascorbic acid/erythorbate to reduce nitrosamine formation

usa.classification: Approved, regulated by USDA and FDA
usa.notes: Ingoing amount: typically 120-200 ppm; must be used with sodium/potassium erythorbate or ascorbate to inhibit nitrosamine formation

japan.notes: Strict limits enforced; residual limits similar to EU standards

australia_nz.notes: Approved with strict maximum residue limits

canada.notes: Regulated with specific maximum levels for different meat products

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–0.07 mg/kg bw/day (nitrite ion - group ADI with E249)

regulatory_history.event: Nitrites/nitrates used for thousands of years in meat preservation (before chemistry understood)
Research identifies nitrosamine formation from nitrites during high-heat cooking
USA mandates addition of ascorbic acid/erythorbate to nitrite-cured meats to reduce nitrosamine formation
IARC classifies processed meat as Group 1 carcinogen; nitrites identified as contributing factor
JECFA and EFSA re-evaluate nitrites; maintain ADI but emphasize consumption moderation and nitrosamine mitigation
Research continues on safer alternatives; 'nitrite-free' products often contain higher levels from celery powder/natural sources

adi_context.source_note: CRITICAL CONTROVERSY - Risk vs. Benefit Balance: THE BAD: Forms carcinogenic nitrosamines when cooked at high heat; IARC classified processed meat as Group 1 carcinogen (2015); linked to colorectal and stomach cancer. THE GOOD: Prevents deadly botulism (Clostridium botulinum - fatality rate up to 10%); critical food safety function. THE DILEMMA: 'Nitrite-free' bacon often contains HIGHER levels from celery powder. CURRENT CONSENSUS: Benefits outweigh risks when used properly; limit consumption; vitamin C/E added to block nitrosamine formation. Unlike many additives, nitrites serve CRITICAL safety function, not just cosmetic.
